steps.title: Find the key signal
steps.content: Text 1 claims social structures progressed linearly: from equal-status hunter-gatherer bands $\Rightarrow$ through agriculture-driven population growth $\Rightarrow$ to hierarchical stages (clans, chiefdoms, states). Graeber and Wengrow's response hinges on whether they accept or challenge this linear progression.

steps.title: Apply the signal to the choices
steps.content:
Graeber and Wengrow directly challenge the linear progression framework—their evidence of hierarchy 50,000 years ago shows social systems have always been flexible, not progressing through stages.

  • A: Says G&W concede hierarchy but rank decentralized systems as more significant. G&W don't rank one over the other—they argue humans alternate between both.
  • B: Says G&W dispute the linear, stage-by-stage progression. This matches their core claim that hunter-gatherers showed flexible, hierarchical structures 50,000 years ago.
  • C: Says G&W agree hierarchy didn't exist before agriculture. Their 50,000-year-old evidence of esteemed individuals directly contradicts this.
  • D: Says G&W challenge whether hunter-gatherers were the earliest social form. They never dispute this—they dispute the progression that followed.
